front: Building: Paris cultural complex with color-coded exterior pipes — blue for air, green for water, yellow for electricity, red for elevators/escalators — co-designed with Richard Rogers
Building: Piano was the alphabetically-first of its two co-designers
Building: named for a former French president; houses the French National Museum of Modern Art, a public library, and the music research center IRCAM
Building: design inspired by Archigram's 'Plug-in City' and Cedric Price's Fun Palace
Building: Stravinsky Fountain nearby features 16 sculptures by Niki de Saint-Phalle and Jean Tinguely, including kinetic figures of a firebird and a fox
Building: called a 'Trojan Horse' by Sanford Kwinter; engineered with 9-ton cast-steel 'gerberettes' to support long trusses
Building: Gordon Matta-Clark made 'Conical Intersect' to critique the gentrification caused by its construction
Building: Nam June Paik broadcast 'Good Morning, Mr. Orwell' from this building
Building: tallest building in the United Kingdom; a 95-story glass-clad pyramidal skyscraper in London
Building: its steep pyramidal shape was inspired by Canaletto's paintings of London's spires
Building: built on the site of the demolished Southwark Towers; Qatar owns a 95% share
Building: airport terminal built on the artificial island of Kankujima in Osaka Bay, Japan; features the world's longest airport terminal — a mile-long departure lounge
Building: airport terminal — sliding joints in its wing-shaped structure helped it survive the 1995 Kobe earthquake unscathed; collaborator was Noriaki Okabe

back: Whitney Museum of American Art (2015) (Renzo Piano)

front: Building: New York museum in the Meatpacking District that replaced a Marcel Breuer-designed building; nine-story terraced structure opening onto the High Line
back: Whitney Museum of American Art (2015) (Renzo Piano)

front: Building: features a cantilevered entrance leading to a 'largo'; matches the industrial character of its Meatpacking District location
back: Whitney Museum of American Art (2015) (Renzo Piano)

front: Building: Noumea, New Caledonia cultural center celebrating Kanak culture; ten pavilions ('cases') inspired by traditional Kanak Great Houses, divided into three 'villages'
Building: uses specially designed louvers called 'nacos' on the exterior to regulate wind; features programmable glass louvers and iroko wood slats imported from Africa
Building: Houston museum with ferroconcrete 'leaves' (ferrous leaf roofing) on the roof that serve as heat source and UV protection to maximize natural light; contains a Cy Twombly Pavilion

back: Modern Wing, Art Institute of Chicago (2009) (Renzo Piano)
work: Modern Wing, Art Institute of Chicago (2009)

front: Building: Art Institute of Chicago addition in Grant Park; features aluminum sunshade nicknamed 'The Flying Carpet'; connected to Millennium Park by the Nichols Bridgeway
back: Modern Wing, Art Institute of Chicago (2009) (Renzo Piano)
work: Modern Wing, Art Institute of Chicago (2009)

front: Building: Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum visitor center in Boston, connected to the original building by Michael Van Valkenburgh's winding path through the Monk's Garden
back: Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum Expansion (2012) (Renzo Piano)

front: Building: Zentrum Paul Klee in Bern — wavy steel beams designed to blend into surrounding hills; published Paul Klee's 'Writings on Form and Design Theory'
Building: California Academy of Sciences in San Francisco — green roof planted with dudleyas, inspired by the city's seven major hills
Building: Fogg Museum renovation at Harvard — preserved the original brick facade but added a glass pyramid roof
Building: Morgan Library expansion — added three glass-and-steel 'modernist entrance' pavilions to the McKim Building
Building: Parliament House of Malta — Gozitan limestone slabs carved to appear eroded; zero-emission design
Building: Padre Pio Pilgrimage Church — attached convent has bare concrete walls, a hidden slit admitting light, a concrete vaulted oratory, and orange-walled cells
Architect: Italian Pritzker Prize winner; trademark use of natural light in museum designs
Architect: converted a gigantic automobile manufacturing plant into a convention center and trade fair district
